hard.next_step: 6/25 Steve Ovadje : Schedule discovery call with solutions consultant on 7/1 '26 4pm Central
hard.ais_why: Notable interest in AI-driven features that streamline procurement (Slack-integrated intake/orchestration, conversational features); Agentic-platform flag set to Yes; current Coupa described as convoluted, manual, and not scalable; but budget not discussed and Ben is an evaluator without confirmed authority.

north_star_verdict.headline: Brand-new mid-market deal (10 days old) in early Qualified stage: Ben Slaugh ran a strong intake-focused discovery on 1 July and self-booked a tailored demo for 9 July, so engagement is live, but he is a single-threaded, self-declared non-decision-maker and the primary threat is do-nothing (reconfigure Coupa) rather than a rival.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 26 Jun 2026, moved Initial Interest -> Qualified 29 Jun 2026, swept 6 Jul 2026 (10 days old, 7 days in Qualified). Days to close: 147 (close 30 Nov 2026). Last real two-way buyer touch: 1 Jul 2026 discovery call; demo scheduled 9 Jul 2026 (LastActivityDate). No forward slip. Pace: enterprise/mid-market motion allows 60-90 days for discovery, demos and RFI/RFP positioning before Shortlisted; at 7 days in with 147 remaining the deal is early and on schedule.
evidence: Deal created 26 Jun 2026, qualified 29 Jun 2026, 10 days old at 6 Jul 2026 sweep.
Stage Qualified, forecast Pipeline, amount $150k, close 30 Nov 2026 (147 days out).
Live two-way discovery call 1 Jul 2026 with buyer Ben Slaugh; intake/orchestration scope defined.
Ben self-selected a demo slot: Thursday 9 Jul 2026, 10am Central, 1 hour (Avoma, 1 Jul).
Single-thread and early: Ben confirmed 'it'll just be me' for the 9 Jul demo; no EB, budget, or timeline yet.
Pipeline forecast is appropriate for a 10-day-old Qualified deal in active discovery.

metrics.narrative: On the 1 July 2026 discovery call Ben Slaugh (Business Relationship Management) framed the problem operationally, not financially: roughly 70% of his team's requests are non-catalog, non-onboarded-supplier intake handled today through Jira, which he called 'more of a workaround than a solution.' The sharpest stake he cited was manual goods receipt: 'an order for $60,000,000 worth of GPUs comes in' and the invoice, not a receipt process, is 'the tickler' for someone to confirm delivery by hand. No cost, cycle-time, headcount, or savings figure was attached to the intake pain, and budget was never discussed. Move: on the 9 July demo, convert the manual-workaround pain into numbers (monthly non-catalog request volume, hours lost, rework/error cost) to build a defensible case for a $150k platform.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is engaged as of 6 July 2026. Ben Slaugh is explicit that he lacks authority: 'I'm not the final decision maker here by any means... I understand what the problem is on the ground level' (1 July call). The person actually steering the evaluation is Mohammad Rasheed, Director of Procurement, Corporate Services, whom Ben named as the source of the strategy ('the feedback I got from Mohammad is that we're taking a two-pronged approach'), but Mohammad has never joined a call and his budget authority is unconfirmed. Budget was not discussed at all on either call. Move: get Ben to name who owns the intake budget and secure Mohammad (or a VP above him) into the next session.
sources: OpportunityContactRole: Ben Slaugh (Evaluator), Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement)

decision_criteria.narrative: Ben stated the core buying criterion plainly on 1 July: 'Zycus is not a bolt on alternative. It's a full rip and replace of Coupa, and to make that argument persuasive, I need to see that you can do the orchestration better than Coupa.' The specific criteria are AI-driven exception management for non-catalog / non-onboarded-supplier requests ('give me a reason why you wanna go outside of that supplier base, and then when do I get a real human involved'), orchestration across the existing stack, integration with Ironclad (contract repository) and whatever P2P remains, and ideally Slack-embedded intake. These are confirmed from the call; there is no formal RFP or scorecard yet ('we aren't using an RFP tool of any formal knowledge'). Move: turn Ben's stated criteria into an explicit fit-checklist he can carry internally.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer is running an informal, bottom-up build-the-case process, not a structured RFP. Ben positioned himself as the ground-level scout: 'I'm here as a kind of first early look at what you guys do to then tell others internally, like, we should take a stronger look at Zycus.' The strategy is two-pronged and set by Mohammad Rasheed: replace the Jira intake bolt-on now, or, longer term and 'a bigger project,' rip and replace Coupa entirely, but only 'if we really see a marked improvement.' No approval chain, evaluation timeline, or committee has surfaced. Move: map the internal path (who Ben reports the demo to, who signs off on scope, when) on the 9 July call.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics (MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec) are unknown as of 6 July 2026, which is expected for a 10-day-old Qualified deal in discovery. No paper-process, security-review, or implementation-partner discussion has occurred. What we do know about the buyer's environment: they use Ironclad as their contract repository and route legal reviews through it, so any solution 'would ideally be integrating with that.' Visibility will come once the deal moves past demo into commercials; the move is to ask Ben to walk through Crusoe's typical software-procurement and security-review process at that point.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is well-articulated and confirmed by Ben on 1 July. Intake for non-catalog / non-onboarded-supplier requests (about 70% of his team's volume) is run through Jira as a poor workaround; contract workflows started from Coupa 'don't work' for pre-approved vendors because 'they're not in Coupa'; supplier onboarding uses a form that is 'too broad and intimidating' with no way to tag a simple-vs-long form; and goods receipt is fully manual (the $60M GPU example). Ben also wants AI to filter requests and decide when to pull in humans (sourcing, IT, legal). The pain owner is Ben in his strategic-sourcing capacity, with the onboarding team as a secondary owner ('I'm aware of their headache'). This is a genuine, multi-symptom pain, not a vague dissatisfaction.

champion.narrative: Ben Slaugh is a developing champion, not a confirmed one. What has developed: he initiated the evaluation, invested two calls, framed the internal narrative, and stated intent to advocate ('to then tell others internally, like, we should take a stronger look at Zycus'). What is missing: he is explicitly not the decision maker, he has not introduced us to Mohammad Rasheed or any budget owner, he will attend the 9 July demo alone ('it'll just be me'), and he hedges on the case ('we might just be configured wrong in the way we're doing that' with Coupa). So he is an engaged, honest evaluator with advocacy intent but no proven advocacy yet. Move: earn a Zycus preference and an introduction to Mohammad by making the 9 July demo land hard on his exact exception-management pain.

competition.narrative: Coupa is the incumbent platform (procurement, expense, invoicing, punch-outs, onboarding), with Jira bolted on for intake; both are what Zycus would displace. But the single strongest current threat is do-nothing / reconfigure-Coupa-in-place, not a rival vendor. Ben conceded on 1 July that replacing Coupa 'might be a bigger project' because of 'other tag on integrations,' that 'we might just be configured wrong,' and that 'even our own policies of the way we're set up with Coupa could need to change, and we might alleviate some of this.' No third-party competitor (SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, Zip) was named; the buyer even uses Claude and Gemini only to draft RFPs, not as procurement tools. How we win: prove Merlin Intake orchestration and exception management deliver a 'marked improvement' Coupa cannot reach by reconfiguration, and de-risk the switch with an intake-first phased path.

deal_movement.summary: A brand-new deal that stood up fast: created 26 Jun, owner reassigned from Angela DSouza to Steve Ovadje and qualified 29 Jun, two discovery calls (25 Jun, 1 Jul), and a demo self-scheduled by the buyer for 9 Jul. Engagement is live and rep-led; the buyer is responsive but single-threaded and pre-budget.

items.change: Owner reassigned Angela DSouza -> Steve Ovadje; stage Initial Interest -> Qualified
First discovery call: Discussion with Zycus + Crusoe Energy (Angela DSouza, Steve Ovadje)
Second discovery call with solutions consultant Srishti; scope defined as intake/orchestration-first
Buyer self-booked a tailored intake demo for Thursday 10am Central

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is do-nothing: reconfiguring Coupa in place rather than switching, which Ben openly floated on 1 Jul ('we might just be configured wrong'). Coupa is the incumbent platform being evaluated for replacement (medium threat, high switching friction from expense/invoicing/punch-out integrations), and Jira is the incumbent intake tool Zycus would displace. No third-party rival vendor has been named as of 6 Jul 2026, so this is a Coupa-replacement-vs-status-quo evaluation, not a multi-vendor bake-off.

competitors.how_we_win: Prove Merlin Intake exception-management and AI orchestration deliver a 'marked improvement' a Coupa reconfiguration cannot, so inertia is not the cheaper default.

competitors.how_we_win: Lead intake-first to sidestep the rip-and-replace risk, then show Merlin orchestrating across Coupa/remaining P2P before proposing full displacement.

competitors.how_we_win: Position Merlin Intake as the purpose-built orchestration layer Jira was never meant to be.

best_practice_check.summary: Win-strategy levers for an early, single-threaded, do-nothing-exposed discovery deal.
flags: Multi-thread to Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement), who owns the scope and strategy; Ben is a single-threaded, self-declared non-decision-maker.
Neutralize do-nothing: prove Merlin Intake beats a Coupa reconfiguration, since Ben floated 'we might just be configured wrong' as the cheaper alternative.
Win the 9 Jul demo on Ben's exact pain (non-catalog exception routing, AI human-in-the-loop), not a broad product tour, and give him internal proof to advocate.

Lead the 9 Jul demo with Merlin's agentic orchestration to land the differentiation.

critical_signals.text: The real contest is inertia, not a rival: Ben conceded Coupa may just be misconfigured and a full replacement is a 'bigger project,' so the status quo can win by default.
The 1 July discovery reframed the deal to intake-first orchestration and produced a buyer-booked demo for 9 July, but Ben will attend alone.
Mohammad Rasheed, Director of Procurement, is directing the scope from behind the scenes but has never joined a call and remains unmet.
Slack-embedded intake surfaced as a buyer need, and Zycus offers only Teams today with Slack on the roadmap.
No budget, price, or authority discussed; Ben lacks buying authority and could not point to a budget owner.

customer_preference.evidence: Neutral and exploratory: Ben is here for a 'first early look,' hedges that Coupa 'might just be configured wrong,' and needs to see orchestration beat Coupa before advocating; no Zycus-specific preference stated (1 Jul).

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only: ~70% non-catalog intake via a Jira workaround, manual goods receipt on a $60M GPU order, intimidating onboarding form; no cost, savings, or ROI figure and no budget (1 Jul).

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A 10-day-old mid-market deal where Ben Slaugh, a ground-level evaluator who says he is not the decision maker, is scoping an intake/orchestration replacement for Coupa+Jira; the only thing standing up the deal is his own internal pitch, and the field has already narrowed to intake-first with the budget owner Mohammad Rasheed never on a call.

win_position.text: We are early but genuinely in the game: Ben brought us in for a 'first early look' at whether Zycus can orchestrate intake better than Coupa, and a tailored intake demo is booked for 9 July (Avoma, 1 Jul).
Ben is not the buyer: he said 'I'm not the final decision maker here by any means' on 1 July, and the person shaping scope, Mohammad Rasheed, has never joined a call.
The primary threat is do-nothing: Ben conceded Coupa 'might just be configured wrong' and a full replacement is a 'bigger project,' so status quo can win by inertia (1 Jul).

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is real for a 10-day-old deal: two discovery calls (25 Jun, 1 Jul) and a demo Ben self-selected for Thursday 9 July at 10am Central (Avoma, 1 Jul).
It is still single-thread, generic-demo stage: Ben confirmed 'it'll just be me' for the 9 July demo and no pricing, security, or timeline topics have opened.

customer_commitment.text: Commitment is thin: no budget, no decision timeline, and no economic buyer named; Ben is exploring 'to then tell others internally' rather than driving a purchase (Avoma, 1 Jul).
Scope narrowed to intake-first, a phased, cost-conscious posture ('replace Jira intake' now, full Coupa displacement a 'bigger project' later), signalling caution not conviction (1 Jul).

deal_risk.text: Single-thread on a non-decision-maker: if Ben's internal pitch to Mohammad and leadership does not land, the deal has no other advocate (Avoma, 1 Jul).
Do-nothing / reconfigure-Coupa is the competitor, not another vendor: Ben openly questioned whether the intake problem is just a Coupa misconfiguration (1 Jul).

seniority.evidence: Single-threaded on Ben, a self-declared non-decision-maker; Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement) never on a call.

commitment.evidence: No budget, no EB, exploratory 'first early look'; buyer defers a purchase decision to others.

do_nothing_risk.evidence: Ben floated reconfiguring Coupa in place instead of switching; full replacement called a 'bigger project.'

scope_change.from: Full source-to-pay suite implied by the opportunity name and product list (iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake)
scope_change.to: Intake / orchestration first, with sourcing, eProc and the rest phased later
scope_change.detail: On 1 July Ben narrowed the live scope to intake: 'the main focus is intake,' those other products are 'things we'd get into later.' Mohammad's 'two-pronged approach' replaces the Jira intake bolt-on now and defers full Coupa displacement as 'a bigger project.' A defensive, cost-conscious, phased posture.
